# US Economic News — May 2026: Tariffs, Trade Policy, Recession Risk & GDP

## Executive Summary

As of early May 2026, the US economy faces a complex convergence of pressures: a Supreme Court ruling that struck down most of Trump's tariffs (triggering a chaotic refund process), replacement tariffs enacted under new legal authority, an ongoing war with Iran that has closed the Strait of Hormuz and sent energy prices soaring, a weakened US dollar, and mounting federal debt that has surpassed 100% of GDP. Despite these headwinds, Q1 2026 GDP came in at 2.0% annualized growth, though economists warn of a sharp slowdown to ~1% in Q2.

---

## 1. Tariffs & Trade Policy

### Supreme Court Tariff Ruling (February 20, 2026)
- The US Supreme Court **struck down most of President Trump's tariffs** as unconstitutional/illegal in a landmark February 20, 2026 ruling.
- This triggered an obligation for US Customs to refund billions of dollars in duties illegally collected from importers.
- The Trump administration **immediately began rolling out replacement tariffs** under new legal justifications.

### Tariff Refund Process
- US Customs launched an **online refund portal on April 20, 2026**.
- As of **April 26**, Customs had accepted claims covering about **one-fifth of shipments** for which refunds were owed.
- More than **one-third of filed claims were rejected** for technical or data errors (though importers can refile).
- More than **two-thirds of importers were not ready** to file — many of them small businesses.
- The libertarian **Cato Institute warned** that "the federal government will likely keep tens of billions of dollars it should have returned to importers."
- Companies like **Costco and Revlon** preemptively filed lawsuits to stake their refund claims.

### Chinese EV Tariffs
- The **100% tariff on Chinese EVs** (originally imposed by Biden ~2024) remains in place with bipartisan support.
- Despite tariffs, Chinese EVs are entering the US informally through **cross-border traffic from Mexico** where Chinese brands have established dealerships.
- Automaker executives are "resigned to the idea" that Chinese EVs will eventually enter the US market.

### US-Canada Trade
- Trump approved a **major new Canada-US oil pipeline**.

---

## 2. GDP & Economic Growth

### Q1 2026 GDP
- **US GDP grew at 2.0% annualized** in January–March 2026 (Commerce Department, April 30, 2026).
- This was a significant improvement from **0.5% growth in Q4 2025**, which was depressed by a six-week government shutdown.
- **Government spending** rebounded, rising at 4.4% in Q1.
- **Personal spending** rose at 1.6% annual rate.
- **Business investment** in data centers and AI provided a major boost.
- The **housing market** remained in a slump; residential GDP was "pretty weak."

### Forward-Looking Indicators
- Pantheon Macroeconomics economist Oliver Allen said: **"Even before the energy shock was fully felt, underlying growth was already quite weak."**
- Allen forecasts growth could **slow to just 1%** in Q2 2026.
- The top 20% of earners (>$175K/year) account for **60% of all personal spending** (per Moody's Mark Zandi), making the economy "top heavy."
- Tax refund cushion (on average ~$330 larger than last year) helped sustain spending in March but will fade.

### Federal Debt Milestone
- Federal debt held by the public has **exceeded $39 trillion** and now surpasses **100% of GDP** (~$31 trillion GDP vs. ~$31.05 trillion debt).
- This is the first time since just after WWII that debt has exceeded 100% of GDP (excluding a brief pandemic-era moment).
- **Interest on the federal debt exceeds $1 trillion per year** — more than defense or Medicare spending.
- Last year's deficit was **$1.6 trillion**.

---

## 3. Energy Prices & Iran War Impact

### The Iran War (began February 28, 2026)
- The US and Israel launched attacks on Iran on **February 28, 2026**.
- The **Strait of Hormuz has been closed**, disrupting global oil and gas trade.
- Iran submitted a **14-point response** to a US proposal to end the war (May 2, 2026); negotiations ongoing.
- The war's total cost tag has been estimated at **$25 billion** as of late April.

### Oil & Gas Prices
- Oil peaked at **over $125/barrel** last week — a **4-year high**.
- Average US gasoline price: **$4.446/gallon** (May 3, 2026), up from $4.099 one week earlier, and $2.98 on February 26 (two days before the war).
- A year ago, average gas was **$3.171/gallon**.
- Gas prices are the **highest since late July 2022**.
- Global oil supply is down about **2 million barrels/day** from prior IEA estimates.
- Pre-war consumption: ~104 million barrels/day; current: ~102 million barrels/day.
- The **UAE announced it is leaving OPEC** to pump more oil.
- **OPEC+ 7 countries** agreed to increase production by **188,000 barrels/day** starting June.
- The US released **17.5 million barrels from the Strategic Petroleum Reserve** between March 20 and April 24.

### Inflation & Consumer Impact
- Annual inflation jumped to **3.5% in March** (Commerce Department).
- Personal spending grew faster than personal income in March — some consumers borrowing to stay afloat.
- Average credit card balance: **~$6,500**.
- The **US dollar depreciated about 10%** from early January 2025 to end of April 2026.
- The biggest dollar losses in the first half of 2025 were the **worst since 1973** (Morgan Stanley).

---

## 4. Recession Risk

### Expert Assessments
- **Kevin Book (ClearView Energy Partners)**: If gas prices were to fall quickly, the reason "would probably be a bad one — recession, undercutting demand, knocking the knees out from under the market."
- **Mark Zandi (Moody's Analytics)**: The economy is being propped up by wealthy consumer spending and tax refunds; he questions sustainability for lower-income consumers.
- **Oliver Allen (Pantheon Macroeconomics)**: Expects discretionary spending cuts by May/June if gas stays above $4/gallon, which would drag on the broader economy.
- NPR headline: **"Iran war shakes global economy as energy costs surge and recession fears grow"** (May 1, 2026).
- The Hormuz crisis is described as **"the biggest disruption in the history of modern energy"** (S&P Global analyst, per NPR/WBUR).

### Federal Reserve
- **Fed Chairman Jerome Powell** held his last news conference as chair and announced he would **remain on the Fed's governing board** (beyond his chairmanship expiring) to safeguard Fed independence from political pressure.
- **Kevin Warsh** appears headed for Senate confirmation as the next Fed chair.
- The Fed **did not change interest rates** at its latest meeting.
- Powell noted "inflation's getting worse, not better" partly due to the Iran war energy shock.
- The Fed is in a "tricky environment" — inflation worsening but the economy needs support.

### Business Distress Signals
- **Spirit Airlines ceased operations** on May 2, 2026, after failed bailout talks with the Trump administration.
- Spirit had sought a **$500 million federal bailout**.
- Rising jet fuel costs (from the Iran war) combined with long-standing competitive pressures proved fatal.
- Spirit had filed for bankruptcy **twice since 2024**.

---

## 5. Key Risks & Uncertainties

| Risk Factor | Status |
|---|---|
| Iran War duration | Ongoing; ceasefire negotiations active but unresolved |
| Strait of Hormuz | Closed; could remain so for weeks/months |
| Oil price trajectory | Rising; could go higher if strait stays closed |
| Tariff policy | Old tariffs struck down; new tariffs imposed under different legal authority |
| Tariff refund completion | Partial; billions may never be refunded to small importers |
| Consumer spending sustainability | At risk as tax refund cushion fades and gas prices stay high |
| Federal debt trajectory | Exceeding 100% of GDP and growing |
| Dollar weakness | ~10% depreciation over 16 months |
| Fed leadership transition | Powell staying on board; Warsh incoming as chair |

---

*Data sourced primarily from NPR reporting (May 1–4, 2026), citing Commerce Department, AAA, IEA, Morgan Stanley, Moody's Analytics, Pantheon Macroeconomics, ClearView Energy Partners, Cato Institute, TransUnion, and US Customs data.*

key_facts: The US Supreme Court struck down most of Trump's tariffs on February 20, 2026; the administration immediately imposed replacement tariffs under new legal authority.
US Q1 2026 GDP grew at 2.0% annualized rate (up from 0.5% in Q4 2025, which was depressed by a government shutdown), per Commerce Department April 30, 2026.
Pantheon Macroeconomics forecasts Q2 2026 GDP growth could slow to just 1% due to the energy shock.
The Iran war (began February 28, 2026) has closed the Strait of Hormuz, sending oil to a 4-year high above $125/barrel.
US average gasoline price reached $4.446/gallon on May 3, 2026 — up from $2.98 on February 26, 2026 (two days before the war).
Annual US inflation jumped to 3.5% in March 2026 (Commerce Department).
Federal debt exceeded $39 trillion and surpassed 100% of GDP for the first time since WWII.
Interest on federal debt now exceeds $1 trillion per year — more than defense or Medicare.
The US dollar depreciated about 10% from early January 2025 to end of April 2026, with first-half 2025 losses the worst since 1973 (Morgan Stanley).
The tariff refund portal opened April 20; as of April 26, only ~20% of owed shipment refunds had accepted claims; over one-third of claims were rejected for errors.
The Cato Institute warns the government will likely keep tens of billions in tariff refunds it promised courts to return.
The US released 17.5 million barrels from the Strategic Petroleum Reserve between March 20 and April 24, 2026.
Global oil supply is down ~2 million barrels/day from prior IEA estimates; demand fell from 104M to 102M bpd.
OPEC+ 7 countries agreed to increase production by 188,000 barrels/day starting June 2026; the UAE announced departure from OPEC.
Spirit Airlines ceased operations May 2, 2026 after failed $500M bailout talks — a casualty of soaring jet fuel costs and structural competitive pressures.
Fed Chairman Powell announced he will remain on the Fed board after his chairmanship ends to safeguard Fed independence; Kevin Warsh is expected to become next Fed chair.
The Fed held rates steady, caught between worsening inflation and potential economic slowdown.
The top 20% of earners (>$175K/year) account for 60% of all US personal spending (Moody's Mark Zandi).
The 100% tariff on Chinese EVs remains in place with bipartisan support, though Chinese EVs are entering the US informally through cross-border traffic from Mexico.
ClearView Energy Partners warns that if gas prices fall quickly, it would likely signal recession rather than positive resolution.
